lesson.menu_desc: Telling the story of your life with narrative tenses -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 2 -- Chapters & Turning Points
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Pricila Adamo | Aula 2 -- Chapters & Turning Points

reading.rtitle: The Year Everything Changed: How One Decision Rewrote a Life
paras: By the time Marina turned fifty, she had spent nearly three decades running the same small bakery her grandmother had founded. She had survived recessions, she had buried a marriage, and she had quietly told herself that the most interesting chapters of her life were already behind her. In hindsight, she would call that the most pivotal year of all.

It began with a setback. A fire had gutted the kitchen one winter night, and by the time the insurance had paid out, she had already decided she would not rebuild. The decision felt daunting, even reckless. But she had been dreaming for years of something different, and the fire, oddly, had paved the way. Trusting little more than a gut feeling, she embarked on a year of travelling the world to learn the breads of other cultures.

Against all odds, she came back transformed. The woman who had once believed her story was finished had, in fact, only reached an intermission. She had put the old life behind her and opened a new bakery -- braver, wiser and, by every measure, more rewarding than the one before. The fire, she now says, was not the end of her story. It was the turning of a page.
reading.source: Adapted from a profile, Reader's Companion

gist.prompt: What is the writer's main point about Marina's story?
choices: Losing the bakery to the fire was a tragedy that ended the most interesting part of her life.
A painful setback became the turning point that led her to a braver, more rewarding chapter.
Marina should never have given up the bakery her grandmother had founded.

items: Before the fire, Marina believed her most interesting chapters were still ahead of her.
f
She had told herself they were already behind her.
By the time the insurance paid out, she had already decided not to rebuild.
t
The text says exactly this -- a past perfect 'had decided' before the payout.
She had been dreaming of something different for years before the fire.
t
The text says she had been dreaming for years.
The new bakery turned out to be less rewarding than the old one.
f
It was more rewarding by every measure.
Marina calls the fire a turning point in hindsight. Looking back, which moment in your own life turned out to be more important than it seemed at the time?
She trusted little more than a gut feeling. When has a gut feeling led you to a good decision?
The writer says her story had only reached an intermission, not an ending. How does that idea sit with your own sense of this chapter of your life?
Think of a setback you have put behind you. What did you learn from how you overcame it?
Scenario 1
Tell a friend about a decision you had already made before an event forced your hand. Use the past perfect: 'By the time..., I had...'
Scenario 2
Describe a setback you faced and how you eventually overcame it. Sequence the events clearly with past simple and past perfect.
Scenario 3
Recall a pivotal moment from your twenties. Explain what had happened before it, and what changed afterwards.
Scenario 4
Talk about a leap of faith you took. Say what you had been thinking for a while before you finally embarked on it.

rephrase.title: Combine each pair using the past perfect
items: The fire happened. Then she decided not to rebuild.
By the time
She dreamed of change for years. Then she embarked on her trip.
had been
She put the old life behind her. Then she opened a new bakery.
After she had
I finished my career. Then I started travelling.
Once I had
We saved for years. Then we finally took the leap.
had been

answerkey.title: Reveal the rephrase answer key
list: By the time the fire happened, she had decided not to rebuild.
She had been dreaming of change for years before she embarked on her trip.
After she had put the old life behind her, she opened a new bakery.
Once I had finished my career, I started travelling.
We had been saving for years before we finally took the leap.
answerkey.note: Form check: past perfect = had + past participle (the earlier action); past perfect continuous = had been + -ing (an earlier ongoing action). The later action stays in the past simple.

analyse.title: How the writer builds the story
items: Find two sentences that use the past perfect to show what had happened BEFORE the main events.
The writer waits until the end to reveal the meaning of the fire. How does delaying that change the impact of the story?
In your own words, what is the difference between an 'ending' and an 'intermission', and why does the writer choose the second word?

extra_audio.text: For years, she had been dreaming of a change she could not yet name. The early setbacks had felt daunting, and she nearly gave up. Trusting a gut feeling, she took a leap of faith and stepped back from the clinic. Against all odds, the move paved the way for a far more rewarding chapter. In hindsight, that quiet decision had become the defining moment of her life.

listenings.text: Looking back, I can see now that everything changed in one single year. By the time I turned forty-five, I had been running my clinic for almost two decades, and I had convinced myself that my path was already set. Then came the setback. My closest partner had decided to leave, and at first it felt daunting, even frightening. But in hindsight, that was the most pivotal moment of all. I had been dreaming of slowing down for years, and suddenly the door had opened. So I took a leap of faith. I put the old plan behind me, and I embarked on a completely new chapter -- one that has turned out to be far more rewarding than I ever imagined.

People often ask me what the secret was. Honestly, there was no secret. By the time the big decision came, I had already failed at two other things, and I had learned more from those failures than from any success. Against all odds, those setbacks paved the way. They taught me to trust a gut feeling, to put the past behind me, and to keep moving forward. So when the pivotal moment finally arrived, I was ready. I had been waiting for it, in a way, my whole life. And the chapter that followed was, by far, the most defining of them all.
